coredata.dc:description: Integrating lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as an energy storage system in electronic devices, including electric vehicles (EVs), creates new challenges. LIB design must be optimized according to the specifications of each application to improve battery performance and safety in each application while avoiding rapid battery degradation. Therefore, accurate models that can forecast the performance of LIBs under various operating conditions are required. Consequently, the primary purpose of this study is to establish a battery model that accounts for the correct thermal heating of large prismatic batteries (PBs) used in EVs. Due to large fluctuations in the space current and Temp (Temp) of PCs (PCs), one-dimensional (1D) models cannot be used to model these PCs. This work combines a 1D model into a three-dimensional multilayer model by the resistive network method. The established model was validated by evaluating the simulated surface Temp of a PB (containing LFP as the positive (+ve) electrode and graphite as the negative (-ve) electrode) with experimental data at different charge (CH)/ discharge (DISCH) rates (1C, 2C, 3C, and 5C). Application of the model utilized effect size, location, and thickness of current collector sheets on the electrochemical (electrochem) properties of large cells is evaluated. Moving the bumps from the edge and one side (a standard commercial design) to the center and the other side of the cell and maximizing their width have been shown to reduce the change in imbalance caused by electrochem current flow.

head.ce:title: Development of robust and accurate thermo-electrochemical models for Lithium-ion batteries

ce:author.ce:given-name: Ahmed Abd El Baset Abd El

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as an energy storage system in electronic devices, including electric vehicles (EVs), creates new challenges. LIB design must be optimized according to the specifications of each application to improve battery performance and safety in each application while avoiding rapid battery degradation. Therefore, accurate models that can forecast the performance of LIBs under various operating conditions are required. Consequently, the primary purpose of this study is to establish a battery model that accounts for the correct thermal heating of large prismatic batteries (PBs) used in EVs. Due to large fluctuations in the space current and Temp (Temp) of PCs (PCs), one-dimensional (1D) models cannot be used to model these PCs. This work combines a 1D model into a three-dimensional multilayer model by the resistive network method. The established model was validated by evaluating the simulated surface Temp of a PB (containing LFP as the positive (+ve) electrode and graphite as the negative (-ve) electrode) with experimental data at different charge (CH)/ discharge (DISCH) rates (1C, 2C, 3C, and 5C). Application of the model utilized effect size, location, and thickness of current collector sheets on the electrochemical (electrochem) properties of large cells is evaluated. Moving the bumps from the edge and one side (a standard commercial design) to the center and the other side of the cell and maximizing their width have been shown to reduce the change in imbalance caused by electrochem current flow.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high voltage (>4V/cell), high energy density (265 Wh) (L−1), and longer lifespan, LIBs are frequently employed in EVs, reliable grid-connected energy storage systems, and various other consumer gadgets. The usage of LIBs in the transportation and aerospace sectors has resulted in more giant cells and battery packs with longer ranges and more demanding CH and DISCH cycles. However, thermal instability and Temp-dependent nonlinear behavior frequently hampered LIB systems' safe and dependable functioning. The performance of the LIB, including its longevity, efficiency, dependability, and safety, is directly impacted by using the battery outside of its safe operating Temp. The ideal working Temp range for LIBs, according to studies on their thermal efficiency, is between 25°C and 40°C [1,2]. According to [3], profound charging circumstances and varied charging current requirements can cause the internal and surface Temps to diverge by more than 10°C in real applications. Thermal shock, explosion, and fire can result from an excessive Temp difference and a high-Temp concentration inside the battery [4]. To properly monitor battery parameters (current, voltage, and Temp) and evaluate the battery health state of charge (SOC), state of health (SOH), remaining usable life (RUL), and state of Temp (SOT), a battery management system (BMS) is required [5]. Studies have demonstrated that SOC, SOH, and residual storage capacity are functions of Temp; as a result, the Temp of a battery during charging and discharging significantly impacts the Coulombic efficiency of the battery. Other standard BMS functions include cell balancing and fault detection/diagnosis, frequently requiring knowledge of cell and individual cell Temps for optimal energy usage, operational safety, reliability, and long battery life [6–10]. As a result, accurate core and surface Temp knowledge is crucial for effective thermal control and the security of LIB cells. Heating the battery to the proper limits to ensure adequate performance at low Temps can also drastically diminish battery capacity in areas with a cold climate [11,12]. Additionally, research has revealed that the battery deteriorates by about 5% for every 0.1°C increase outside of the safe operating range [13]. According to the research, the DISCH process—particularly the rapid DISCH process—is when most of the heat is emitted. Therefore, accurate Temp estimation is crucial for good thermal management and safety when the battery is quickly depleted and heated to prevent energy loss.

Precise battery Temp information is unquestionably crucial for LIBs' thermal management and safety. Although each cell can have a Temp sensor placed inside of it to monitor the surface Temp, it is challenging to use a physical probe to measure the inside Temp directly. The thousands of batteries and modules contained within each high-capacity battery used in EVs and grid-connected systems make it impractical to install Temp sensors on every surface of the battery. This has an impact on system cost, footprint, and weight. To create an intelligent battery, researchers have also combined multi-dimensional (MD) sensing and self-healing capabilities into a single battery [14–18]. Intelligent batteries measure variables and evaluate the battery's health, including Temp. Accurate Temp estimation is still required in intelligent batteries despite the modular deployment of BMS, as placing sensors in each cell would increase operational expenses and complexity. To create a low-cost car BMS, researchers tried to create a highly accurate, exact, easy-to-implement, and computationally cheap online Temp estimating algorithm. To measure Temp, academics have put out several different approaches thus far.

Regarding the qualities mentioned above of an ideal BMS, each of the various types of techniques has its benefits and drawbacks. To choose the best technology for a given need, researchers and developers can benefit greatly from having a broad overview of all currently used technologies as a starting point for further investigation. However, there needs to be a synopsis of the literature that outlines the developments and explains the present issues, difficulties, and potential for further study. This work covers the research gap by thoroughly examining the most recent Temp-estimating techniques published in the literature.

Regardless of the battery's chemistry, heat is generated inside the battery during CH/DISCH cycles and while at rest. This heat is generated through chemicals, electrochem, and predominantly exothermic transport processes. Insufficient heat flow from the battery to the surroundings can cause heat to accumulate inside the battery, raising internal and surface Temps and the possibility of thermal shock. Batteries with rigid insulation, which are utilized in EVs and have quick CH/DISCH rates and high operating Temps, are the ones where this effect is most visible. The heat conductivity of cylindrical LIB, frequently utilized in big LIB, could be more substantial. So, exothermic and heat transport models make up a standard heat estimating system [19]. While other models use mathematical forms of battery chemistry to compute heat generation (HG), the Bernard HG model is typically also used to estimate the total heat produced [20]. The battery Temp is a function of the battery condition, so the adaptation algorithm also considers the effects of different battery conditions like SOC and SOH. The predicted total Temp is then used to anticipate the battery Temp using a heat transfer (HT) model and additional outside measurement like ambient Temp. Measured or estimated Temp is used as feedback in feedback scoring systems to increase forecast accuracy. Fig. 1 depicts a schematic representation of a general LIB Temp evaluation scheme [21].

A Temp estimation system typically comprises an at-release transfer model, as shown in Fig. 1. Heat source-based approaches and modeling can be used to broadly categorize the heat release patterns that have been described in the literature. Three types of simulation strategy-based heating models may be identified: equivalent circuit models (ECM), physics-based electrochem models, and black-box models [22–30]. According to various heat sources, these models can be divided into centralized, distributed models, and heterogeneous models [25,31,32]. Geothermal models typically assume that all heat is produced solely in the core, which is assumed to simplify the simulation. While heterogeneity models capture variable HG in distinct cell layers, frequently leading to Temp and current density within the cell, diffusion heating models provide homogenous heating across the cell shape. Heterogeneous models are the most complex and require much modeling expertise, but they are also the most detailed and may make very accurate forecasts. Between centralized and heterogeneous models, the distributed heating model strikes a balance. Thermal resistance models (coupled or dispersed variables), finite element analysis (FEM) models, and data-based techniques are three categories of HT models [27,28,33–40]. The capacitive-resistive thermal model (TM) uses an analogy between electrical and thermal systems. Thermistor resistors can be categorized into several groups, as shown in Fig. 2 . Coupled parameter models are straightforward and appropriate for grid systems. However, they can only forecast one or two average Temp, and the battery distribution in space is not uniform, particularly for cylindrical LIBs with higher capacities. However, due to the difficulty of the calculations, sophisticated distributed models are not appropriate for networks even though they can precisely explain the Temp distribution within cells [41,42]. Other intricate LIB models have been researched, considering the thermal characteristics of many layers. One-state/node models only provide information on the core Temp, whereas two-state/node models provide core and surface Temps [43–48].

Battery heating models are a subset of HT models that accept the total amount of heat produced as an input variable and estimate the total heat produced by the batteries. Researchers used various heat-generating and heat-transfer models to create an estimated heat system.

Consequently, it is challenging to classify various evaluation techniques. Electrochem, thermal modeling-based systems, equivalent circuit model (EECM)-based systems, machine learning (ML)-based systems, numerical modeling-based systems, direct resistivity measurement-based systems, and magnetic nanoparticles-based systems are the main categories of Temp estimation programs. Fig. 2 displays the LIB series' TM, heat transmission model, and method for estimating Temp.

To simulate battery Temps under various operating situations, geometries, or cooling rates, researchers started developing TM in the early 1990s. These models were frequently integrated with electrochem models. From straightforward 1D (radial) models to intricate 3D TMs [35,47–51]. To create mathematical models of the electrochem behavior of batteries, scientists primarily use various analytical techniques. In 1D models, isothermal flow, and the battery's thermophysical characteristics are assumed to have constant heat release rates. Complex 3D models require an in-depth understanding of the thermodynamic properties of battery materials and components—electrode kinetics—to account for Temp changes caused by ohmic resistance, chemical reactions, mixing processes, polarization, and electrical resistance. Very intricate models can be used to estimate Temp with a high degree of accuracy, but these precise models are crucial for battery construction. They are incompatible with low computing resource BMS Temp estimation. These sophisticated models can explain how batteries' nonlinear behavior changes over time. However, they frequently call for different system characteristics and operating settings, necessitating significant experimental testing. However, other characteristics, such as those related to transport, thermodynamics, and thermal impacts, are very challenging to assess.

To calculate the overall heat dissipation during CH/DISCH, Thomas and Newman devised a comprehensive LIB heat dissipation model based on electrochem simulations. Thomas and Newman's basic Equation (Eq) for a LIB battery's overall Temp is [52]:

In formula (1), Q stands for the battery's heating or heat consumption rate, V and U for the battery's equilibrium voltage and potential, respectively, I for the CH or DISCH current, and T for the battery's Temp. Hi is the reaction's change in enthalpy, and ri is its rate. The particle concentration is Cj, and Hj is the partial molar mass of the jth particle. The symbols for time and cell volume are t and v, respectively. Using a "responsible" index, all attributes are based on volume average concentration. While this study did not include Temp estimations, the model can only provide detailed information on Temp. Many other scientists frequently utilize his TM.

Due to the high number of calculations required by the simulation's level of realism, it is complicated and unsuitable for online applications. The popular electrochem model, the Doyle-Fuller-Newman model, is frequently quoted and utilized in TM [35,53]. The internal characteristics of a LIBs are described by a set of algebraic Eqs that are partially nonlinear. A different name for this is synthetic 2D (P2D) modeling. This model's primary flaw is its heavy processing requirement, which restricts its capacity to evaluate the state of a car BMS network. A basic 1D local TM is sufficient for battery design, particularly for large-scale LIB TM modeling [53]. Additionally, it does not thoroughly grasp how different battery parts—like electrodes, electrolytes, and separators—affect HG. Few researchers have investigated pulse power limiting to prevent thermal shock and create thermal management systems using intricate electrochem models [54,55]. They are mostly employed in the creation of LIB batteries and cells. [56] applied a coupled local electrochem-TM to forecast LIB thermal properties and specific electrode properties at various operating Temps. The model only offers outcomes from lab tests, not real-world usage scenarios, and is validated based on experimental data of a stationary hybrid vehicle (HEV) and impulse response. He investigated how the charging current impacted the interior Temp. To create a MD electrochem, TM of LIBs that allowed for a more thorough investigation of thermal properties and thermal conductivity (TC), [57] combined more comprehensive data and battery parameters.

The models' complexity and processing cost prevent them from being applied outside of the BMS, despite the accuracy of the estimates. Not all heat sources are often modeled or considered due to the absence of a thorough understanding of the electrochem processes occurring in LIBs and the accompanying mathematical Eqs to decrease the computing cost. Big mistakes in Temp estimates can result from this non-replicable heating activity. [58] created a two-site TM without prior information on thermal parameters using discrete and inverse modeling techniques. It is not necessary to heat model each heat source because the model can predict the overall heat produced by battery cells. Instead, aberrant heating can be identified using the results. Extra heat and fast-loading circumstances verify the model's validity and dependability. Although this method was created for self-heating batteries, it might also be used with other LIB types. Therefore, an additional study is advised in this case. High accuracy electrochem models and integrated measurements like voltage and current were used by [59] to calculate the battery Temp during CH/DISCH at various C rates. Additionally, to relate terminal voltage to cell Temp and Li+ concentration, they employed a dual ensemble Kalman filter (DEKF), combining individual events' extended dynamics. Accurate lithium (Li+) concentration determination is challenging due to computationally expensive models and complexity. Therefore, it is debatable whether to apply this model to actual network predictions. Marelli and Corno used a dual de-aromatic soft-narrow Kalman filter and a pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) electrochem model to approximate the spatial distribution of Temp in LIBs (DUKF) [60]. The cost of simulation and calculation is very high, and its primary purpose is to estimate Li+ concentration. The technique can be expanded to estimate Temp, however. A 1D spatial electrochem-TM was created by [56] to investigate LIB cells' pulse power restriction and thermal behavior. Table 1 provides rapid access to the Temp estimating techniques based on the electrochem -TM put out by various authors. Any model-based electrochem technique generally has two key drawbacks: simulation complexity and high computational cost, which renders these models unsuitable for online prediction and low-cost airborne BMS.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the PC studied consists of many individual cells connected in parallel. Each element consists of a -ve electrode, a separator, and a +ve electrode surrounded by current collectors. The PB studied in this paper comprises -ve and +ve active materials (AMs) according to graphite and LiFePO4. Aluminum (Fig. 3) is a -ve flow collector. The battery comprises 48 individual cells with a nominal capacity of 20 Ah. Both sides of the flow head are covered with electrodes to reduce material consumption and electrical losses. Therefore, the number of current collectors for a 48-cell aluminum battery is 24, and the number of copper current collectors is 25 as the copper current collectors are located at both ends of the battery block, as shown in 6 elements in Fig. 3. Next, all the layers are sealed in the separator sheets and covered with an envelope. The flow to the prism cells is first divided into 48 cells and then placed in a tabbed current collector. The current entering the flux collector is split by the flux collector inside the plate and then by the AM; the flux current in the AM layer is expected to be measured. However, it should be noted that although the resistance of the metal current collectors is meager, the barrier of the conductor cannot be lost sight of, as it is the source of the three-dimensional distribution of the flux through a layer of AM. Fig. 4 shows the flow distribution in stream collectors and layers during DISCH. As shown, the current collector's current is embedded in the AM layers on either side of the current collector.

Online LIBs consist of -ve electrodes made of lithium foil. However, graphite is commonly used as the -ve electrode in commercial batteries—modification of the fundamental Eqs of the 1D model with porous graphite electrodes. Therefore, Table 2 lists these Eqs for the LIB, which contains graphite as the -ve electrode and LiFePO4 as the +ve electrode. The input to the 1D model is the local average flux density jn and the output change in the potential shell of the AM

Where ϕs is the solid phase potential of the electrode, L is the thickness of the AM layer (separate plus electrode), and RC is the contact resistance between the current collector and the electrode.

In a PC, the charge conservation Eq is employed to the current collectors and a model is utilized to predict the current and voltage distribution. The regional average current densities and potential fluctuations throughout the AM layers are related using 1D electrochem models. The current flow in the model (as shown in Fig. 4) requires accounting for current flow from both sides of the current collectors, except for the two copper collectors located at each end of the Li+ PC. To formulate the Eq for each current collector, the charge conservation Eq is expressed in the following manner, with one of the right-hand side terms set to zero for the two copper collectors at the ends of the Li+ PC.

The coupling between the current and potential distributions is employed to calculate these two variables at each time step, unlike references, which omit the potential gradient along the current collectors [62,63].

The boundary conditions for Eq. (3) are set as follows: all -ve current collector tabs have a zero-reference potential, while all +ve tabs have an equal but varying electrical potential. Additionally, it is presumed that the current, Ii, at the top surface area of a +ve current collector tab, is spatially uniform. However, the current can differ for each tab. The total current flowing through all +ve current collector tabs sums to the total applied PC current (Iapp ).

The remaining boundaries of the current collectors are considered to have a zero-flux condition.

By utilizing the above boundary conditions and Eq. (4), the charge conservation Eq. (3) can be solved to determine the voltage distribution in the PC. Eq. (4) is then used to calculate the distribution of normal current density. Once the current density distribution is known, new 1D model (electrochem variables) values can be calculated. This process is repeated for the next step to update the voltage distribution, normal current density distribution, and electrochem variables until the lower cut-off voltage of the PC (2.4 V) during DISCH or the higher cut-off voltage (3.7 V) during CH is reached.

The temperature distribution is determined by a TM after the current density and electrochem variable distributions in the PC have been computed. The heat conduction Eq. utilized in this model has the required HG terms, material characteristics, and boundary conditions. Initially, the governing Eq. and boundary conditions are introduced, subsequently succeeded by the Eqs. utilized to compute the HG in the AM layers and the current collectors. Finally, the method utilized in Eq. (6) to determine material attributes is given.

It is noteworthy that the AM layers are regarded as a solitary layer, wherein the local temperature gradient perpendicular to the current collector plates is insignificant due to the thinness of these layers and the comparatively elevated TC of the materials they contain [64].

, is influenced by various factors, including the specific surface area of the active particles, the reaction current density at the surface of active particles, the local Temp of the AM layers, and the thickness of the AM layers. This calculation considers various terms, such as reversible entropic HG and the irreversible HG terms, including the reaction HG on the surface of the -ve and +ve electrodes' particle surfaces. Additionally, Joule heating resulting from the movement of electrons and ions in the electrodes and electrolyte, as well as HG arising from contact resistance between the electrodes and current collectors, are also included.

On the other hand, the HG in the current collectors,

, is solely due to ohmic resistance in the current collectors. The formula for this HG rate involves the resistance of the current collectors, the current density, and the Temp of the local environment. It is important to note that these two sources of HG must be calculated separately to obtain an accurate representation of the total HG in the PC.

Where σ cc is the electrical conductivity of the current collector, Φ is the local potential of the collector, and i denotes the -ve or +ve current collector.

It is anticipated that the contact resistance between the separator and the electrodes in the AM layers and between the separator and the casing in the outer layer is low since the electrolyte fills the pores and spaces between the material particles and its TC is equivalent to particles. Consequently, as soon as two layers are coupled in a series arrangement (Fig. 5 (series))., the TC, kser , is established using the following Eq:

When determining the effective TC of a layer within a PC, it is necessary to consider both the layer length in the HT direction (L) and the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the HT direction (A). Since each layer within the PC comprises multiple materials, the volume averaging approach calculates the layer's TC (klayer).

During operation, PCs generate significant heat that alters the battery's Temp and impacts the electrochem modeling parameters. Therefore, close coupling between thermal and electrochem models is essential. To achieve this, Song and Evan's technique is used, which assumes a quasi-steady-state Temp at each time step. This involves solving the electrochem model at a fixed Temp, using the heat produced in the TM to compute a new Temp distribution within the PC, and then determining the electrochem parameters for the new electrochem model time step based on the updated Temp distribution. Fig. 6 illustrates this technique. Moreover, the Temp variation throughout each time step is monitored. If it exceeds 1C at any point, the time step is divided in half and repeated to ensure that the quasi-steady-state assumption holds throughout the simulation.

To accurately model the electrochem behavior of a PC, it is necessary to know the electrochem parameters for the electrodes and separator, such as equilibrium potential and diffusion coefficients. The modeling predicts that the PC will have a low cut-off voltage of 2.4 V and a high cut-off voltage of 3.7 V, which the manufacturer recommends. The equilibrium potential of the electrodes is known to be dependent on Temp, as per literature sources such as the graphite electrode and the LFP electrode [66–68]. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the Temp dependence, respectively [66–68]. It is worth noting that even if the Temp dependence of equilibrium potentials is found to be low in the Temp range of interest, it significantly impacts the HG in the AM layers.

When the model is fitted to the onset-of-DISCH region of the pouch cell voltage data, as shown in Fig. 7, the initial stoichiometry of the LFP (graphite) electrode that is present during CH and DISCH may be determined. By fitting the model to the end-of-DISCH area voltage data, it is also possible to estimate the initial stoichiometry of the LFP (graphite) electrode during CH/DISCH.

To obtain the necessary geometric characteristics of the -ve and +ve double-sided electrodes in a PC, the authors dismantled the cell. They conducted measurements, as shown in Fig. 8 . Using a micrometer, they determined the thickness of each layer in the cell, presented in Table 3 . The electrical conductivity of the current collectors and the density, HC, and TC of the battery materials were also determined and provided in Table 3 for use in thermal modeling. Additionally, Eqs 14 and 15 [57] were employed to estimate the electrical conductivities of the copper and aluminum current collectors, which are expressed in S cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stoichiometry window for graphite and LFP electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochem characteristics of graphite and LFP from the previous half-cell simulation may now be used to simulate a 20 Ah PC utilizing two electrodes. However, to achieve this, it is necessary to foresee the initial stoichiometry of the electrodes. When the PC is initially assembled, the LFP cathode is fully fused, but the graphite (anode) is not. At the first CH of the PC, a fraction of the lithium in the cathode forms an SEI layer on the surface of the graphite particles [69]. The initial stoichiometry of the electrode cannot be determined because it needs to be made clear how much lithium was used to produce the SEI. Moreover, the stoichiometric window of each electrode during complete CH/DISCH is impacted by the ratio of the electrode capacitances [70].

Modeling a PB with a 1D model must be successful since ohmic losses in current collectors, terminals, etc., are insignificant at meager CH/DISCH rates. The current density distribution between current collectors tends to be in proximity to the average value. The isothermal model is deemed appropriate for the simulation as even at low speeds, the heating of the PC is relatively minimal. Because the prismatic element contains 48 elements, the electrode dimensions shown in Fig. 8 provide a total area of 1.44 m2 and 1.37 m2 for the graphite and LFP electrodes, respectively. Graphite electrodes have a 5.1% larger surface area than LFP electrodes to prevent lithium coating on their edges [71–73]. It was established that such surplus graphite areas might be inactive when the ratio between the active areas of the graphite electrode and the LFP was considered a modifiable parameter.

Through the process of fitting the model to the onset-of-DISCH region of the PC voltage data, it becomes feasible to approximate the initial stoichiometry of the LFP (graphite) electrode during DISCH/CH. Likewise, through the process of fitting the model to the voltage data obtained from the end-of-DISCH region, the initial stoichiometric ratio of the LFP (graphite) electrode during CH/DISCH can be deduced. Table 4 presents the initial 157 stoichiometries for DISCH and CH. Finally, the ratio of the graphite and LFP active surface areas is assessed using the position of the stage-2 graphite voltage ramp region when fitted to the model. This curve fit yields a ratio of 1.0 between the graphite and LFP active electrode surface areas, indicating that the graphite electrodes 5.1% bigger physical surface area is inactive and only serves to ensure battery safety.

Fig. 9 shows that a grid with a non-uniform structure is employed to enhance the code's convergence. Because of the higher current density and Temp, the trace is narrower to where the signals are. The mesh gets coarser as one gets farther away from the tab because there are fewer gradients. It is significant to note that although the tabs are not part of the model, they impact the battery's thermal performance. The molds are modeled as heat sources to produce this effect. Ohmic losses cause the terminal to heat up and provide a Temp gradient at the current collector/junction contact.

Even with the simplification of the models mentioned above, the 20 Ah PB still requires considerable calculations, taking more than 12 hours for a simulation with 48 individual cells. The calculations for a limited number of 48-cell PCs showed that the current through the current collector was about the same for all individual cells except two individual cells on either side of the PC. The model is further streamlined by employing only six distinct cells to determine the current distribution, two different cells on either side of the PC, and two typical inner battery cells. This is because determining the current distribution takes up most of the model's processing resources and costs. This simplification reduces the maximum computation time to less than an hour. A 1D electrochemistry procedure dramatically slows down the calculation of the current distribution. Nonetheless, rapid thermal calculations still use all 48 cells. Fig. 10

The validation process of the 20 Ah model involves a comparison between the predicted voltage and temperature with the measured operating voltage and Temp distribution on the surface of the PC. The starting Temp of the PC and the surrounding air were both 24°C. The only variables changed to achieve these fits are the total tab resistance and the contact resistance, Rc, between the current collectors and electrodes. The combined tab resistance is made up of the Ohmic losses in the tab material as well as the contact resistance that results from the combining of the separate PC tabs into a single tab. A punching procedure is used to connect the separate cell tabs. The current collector/electrode contact resistance 160 is calculated by fitting the model to the experiment surface Temps, as will be discussed in more detail in the following paragraphs and is expected to be 1.5×10−4 m2. The estimated total resistance for the tabs is also 3×10−4; this value was obtained by modeling CH and DISCH voltages and observing how shifting the total tab resistance up or down affected the voltage curves. The voltage curves change during DISCH and CH, shown in Fig. 11 . The simulation results and experimental data exhibit a high degree of concurrence across all CH and DISCH operational rates.

Voltage curves change during CH and DISCH, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 11, the results of the simulation closely resemble the experimental voltage profiles, with a relative error of no more than 3% during CH and no more than 4% during DISCH.

Fig. 12 presents a comparison of the results of simulations and experimental data obtained at varying CH/DISCH rates (ranging from 1C to 5C), which demonstrates that the model can accurately predict experimental data for all operating rates. Fig. 12 demonstrates that the rate errors for a given C are nearly identical.

The TM utilized in this study was tested by contrasting experimental data with predictions made by the model for the surface Temp distribution of PCs. It should be noted, however, that the voltage cable is connected to the battery ring using a flush safety connector (Model ID/S6AR-N-S) for safety reasons [74]. These connectors' bolt and nut connections make—a contact resistance loop between the cycler cables and the tabs. Despite not being part of the model, the fins are heated independently; hence a boundary for heat flow has been supplied. The heat source at the collector/reed interface is modeled as this heat flux boundary condition. The neck voltage is measured against the resistance of the embedded plug-in, meaning that the throat impedance is less than the impedance that causes ohmic heating of the ear. The thermal resistance must therefore be determined in addition to the joint's overall resistance, as stated above. Establishing this Temp tolerance is explained in the following paragraph in this section.

Fig. 13 provides an up-close look at the top of the flush mounting plugs. Even though the tabs are not accounted for in the model, ohmic heating is applied to them, and as a result, they serve as a heat flux boundary condition. This heat flux boundary condition is represented as a heat source at the interface between the current collector and tab. The significance of Fig. 13 lies in the observation that the voltage drop resistance is lower than the heat production resistance that is accountable for ohmic heating of the tab; to put it another way, the voltage of the tab is measured before the flush-mounting plug contact resistance.

Two thermocouples were attached to the battery petals to measure the Temp of the battery and compare it to the Temp of the PC surface close to the battery petals to better understand the HT from the petals to the battery. The Temp is significantly higher than a particular location on the cell's surface, which causes a large HT from the terminal to the current collector.

The Temp predicted by the model on the surface of the PCs, particularly close to the plates, was fitted to the actual data to determine the thermal resistance of the +ve and -ve plates. The contact resistance between the electrodes and the current collector was also measured along with these resistances. However, this time, the PC base's surface Temp was focused more closely. It should be mentioned that the HT terms at the coil's surface were described by the emissivity of 0.7 and the HT coefficient of 2 W m−2K−1. In this adaption procedure, the heating resistance values of the +ve and -ve plates at 5C DISCH are 5.0×10−4 Ω and 3.6×10−4 Ω, respectively. The +ve and -ve plate values for the 5C charging method are 6.7×10−4 and 4.8×10−4, respectively. During charging compared to discharging, the +ve terminal's thermal resistance value was higher than the -ve terminal's, consistent with the +ve terminals' most incredible recorded Temp. Because thermistor resistors also serve as signal/jump resistors, they are more significant than typical signal resistors. Also, it has been determined that the electrodes' and the related current collectors' contact resistance is 1.5×10-4 Ω m2. The computational results and the experimental findings are in good agreement. The fact that a constant thermal flow was utilized to model the time-varying HT of the signal can be used to explain some of the disparities between the simulation and the experiment that were seen in points 1, 2, and 3 (top of the battery).

The outcomes of the approach for changing the CH/DISCH rate from 1C to 5C are shown in Table 5 . The predicted thermal resistance is influenced by processing speed, as shown in Table 5. Not all the tab's heat is delivered to the battery's current collector. Conductive cables convey the heat produced to the outside environment. Lower heating resistance means less of the heat produced by the tab is transferred to the battery. Lower thermal resistance results from more heat being lost to the environment at more incredible working speeds, consistent with the findings in Table 5. Also, it should be noted that the +ve heating resistance of the blade at a CH/DISCH rate of 1 C is significantly higher than the blade's overall resistance (1.5×10−4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temp effect on the PC performance
ce:section.ce:para: The total power required for loading and unloading is independent of operating speed. According to Barker, this is unusual for a button cell because the electrodes' electrochem activity decreases as the operating speed rises [75]. Commercial batteries outperform button cells electrochemically due to their larger uniaxial voltage, according to [76,77]. They argue that increasing pressure considerably reduces the contact resistance between the LFP particles and the conductive matrix, improving the performance of the electrode.

This research offers an alternative viewpoint based on the impact of Temp on the electrochem performance of batteries. The capacity of PCs at CH and DISCH rates at 24°C and 5°C is significantly reduced, especially during DISCH, according to isothermal modeling. The electrochem performance of the battery is improved by raising the Temp, as evidenced by repeated isothermal simulations at a higher Temp (45°C) and the same CH/DISCH rate at 5°C, which result in higher final capacity values. The actual cause of this impact is the electrodes' Temp-dependent kinetic and transport characteristics, particularly the LFP electrode's diffusion coefficient. These findings demonstrate that the output voltage of the battery during CH/DISCH is consistent with the predictions of the isotherm simulation at 24°C vs. 5°C given that the battery Temp at the start of CH/DISCH is 24°C to 5°C. While the operating voltage of the PB approaches the isothermal simulation at 45°C, the battery's Temp rises when the PB is charged or discharged. This shows that the enhanced electrochem performance of PCs during operation may be due to self-heating.

HT and distribution in prismatic stacks are covered in this chapter. We analyze the Temp distribution in the PC's core cell (chamber no. 24) and the Temp difference between its surface and center. Moreover, heat production in the AM layer and the anode and cathode current collectors of a conventional battery (central battery). The usage limit of the battery is stated since all studies were conducted at 5°C, which is the rate at which most of the heat is released, and the highest Temp is obtained. Future research will address the impact of ambient Temp on the PC's behavior, which has not yet been covered. The actual laboratory Temp of 24°C was used as the starting point for all simulations.

In the case of a current collector, the HG rate does not fluctuate while the battery is operating because the current passing through the current collector is nearly constant over time. Moreover, the current's value does not change between charging and discharging; only its direction does. As a result, the heat release rate in current collector cell 24 has been demonstrated. The most effective heating rate is seen close to the insert, where the current density is highest, as heating of the current collector is caused by resistance to electron transport. Nevertheless, outside this area, the current collector's other sections barely generate heat.

As opposed to current collector heating, the heat produced in the layers of the AM responds very dynamically to the average current density traveling through the layers, the characteristics of the electrode material, and the local state of the cell's Prismatic charge. The normal current density initially increases towards the edge of the prismatic element's top, gradually dropping as it descends. The normal current density distribution inverts, with a higher normal current density at the bottom, toward the end of the battery life.

When the element's initial Temp and the ambient Temp are both 24°C, As previously indicated, the Temp is higher near the projections than elsewhere, particularly at the battery's base. The current collector flange and HT from the flange to the current collector are the leading causes of this enhanced HG.

These simulations were run with a starting battery at 24°C and 5°C. The cell's center constantly had a higher Temp than the exterior. This is especially obvious towards the top of the prismatic element, where more effective heating rates are present, as discussed above. The Temp differential between the PC's center and its surface appears to be minimal. After the conclusion of the DISCH and CH, for instance, point 1 on the top of the battery does not rise over 2°C. The Temp difference between the stack's surface and center will increase if the simulation runs again with a higher HT coefficient. However, this Temp differential does not surpass 3°C even with HT coefficients as high as 20 Wm−2 K−1. It should be noted that when the battery is charged or discharged once, there is a Temp difference of this kind. However, when the battery is utilized in EVs with constant CH/DISCH cycles, the Temp difference between the center and surface of the battery may be more significant.

A 20 Ah PC electrochem-TM has been created and verified using experimental data for CH/DISCH rates ranging from 1C to 5C. A 1D physics-based electrochem model is integrated with charge conservation and heat diffusion Eqs to represent the distribution of electrochem and thermal parameters in the battery region. The model's precision in forecasting the Temp distribution and battery output voltage at the PB surface supports its applicability to various battery materials and geometries. In this model, a half-cell simulation on identical electrode materials yielded all the electrochem characteristics of graphite and lithium-iron-phosphate electrodes. The remaining parameters were determined using data from measurements or literature, making the PC's computed parameters more physically based.

The impact of Temp increase caused by self-heating on the electrochem parameters of a PC with a 20 Ah capacity is first discussed using the established model. The findings demonstrate that raising the electrode's Temp can enhance their kinetic and transport characteristics, which enhances the electrochem performance of large PCs. Then, a PB's heat conductivity and distribution are examined during charging and discharging. This work also discusses the impact of three-dimensional normal current density distribution on heating, as well as other important issues such as current collector heating and reversible chaotic heating. Finally, simulation findings demonstrate that the Temp differential along the thickness of PCs is insignificant. The cell Temp does not reach 3°C even at high CH/DISCH rates and strong HT coefficients on the PC surface can thus be disregarded.

The PB is modeled by the electrochem-TM used in this study. Creating a module or even a package using this architecture is simple. Nevertheless, since PCs take an hour or so to compute, expanding this work to even higher levels, like 100 different PCs, can lead to incredibly long simulation times. In this case, it is recommended to apply even more simplifications, such as neglecting the distribution of electrochem variables in the active layer.

The electrochem-TM developed in this work was not acquainted to examine the effects of various parameters on the electrochem and thermal behavior of PCs. Because it includes length ranges from nanoscale particles to multi-centimeter cells, the developed model can examine the interplay of multiple phenomena at diverse length scales. For instance, the constructed model makes it straightforward to inspect the effects of the AM particle size and chemical composition on the thermal behavior of PBs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ahmed A. El Baset A. El Halim received his B.Sc., and M.Sc. degrees in electrical power engineering from the Higher Institute of Engineering and Technology and Ain Shams University, Egypt in 2016 and 2021 respectively. He is currently a assistant lecturer in the Department of Energy and Renewable Energy, Egyptian Chinese University. Now he is studying for his Ph.D. at the Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University. His research interests include control engineering, renewable energy, power electronics in power systems, and electric vehicles. He can be contacted at email: ahmedabdelbaset2016@gmail.com, abaset@ecu.edu.eg.

Ehab H. E. Bayoumi received his BSc, MSc and PhD degrees in electrical power engineering from Helwan University, Ain Shams University and Cairo University, Egypt in 1988, 1996 and 2001 respectively. He has been with the Electronic Research Institute (ERI), Cairo, Egypt, since 1990. From 2000 to 2001 he joined LUT, Finland, as a Visiting Researcher. He was appointed as an Assistant Professor at Chalmers University of Technology, Sweden from 2003 to 2005. He Appointed as Associate Professor in ERI Egypt in 2008. In 2014 he was retained as Full Professor in ERI Egypt. Currently, he is a Full Professor in The British University in Egypt. He also served at Yanbu Industrial College, Saudi Arabia and Higher Collages of Technology, United Arab Emirates, University of Eswatini, Eswatini, Egyptian Chinese University, Egypt. His research interests include high-performance ac machines, power quality, switching power converters in smart and micro grids, and nonlinear control applications in power electronics, smart-grid, and electric drive systems. Prof. Bayoumi was appointed as an Editor-in Chief of the International Journal in Industrial Electronics and Drives in 2013. He can be contacted at email: ehab.bayoumi@bue.edu.eg

Walid El-Khattam received the B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees in electrical engineering from Ain Shams University, Cairo, Egypt, in 1991 and 1996, respectively, and the Ph.D. degree in electrical engineering from the University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, Canada, in 2005. He joined the University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, AR, USA, as a research associate in 2005. He worked as a Postdoctoral Fellow for the University of Western Ontario and a researcher for Hydro-One the Ontario province distribution company in Canada from 2005 to 2009. Currently, he is employed as an Associate Professor in the Department of Electrical Power and Machines at Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University. He is also working as a consultant for the Egyptian Electric Utility and Consumer Protection Regulatory “EGYPTERA”, Cairo, Egypt. His area of research is distributed generation performance, renewable energy resources' operation and regulatory aspects related to their integration into the grid. He can be contacted at email: walid.elkhattam@eng.asu.edu.eg.

Amr M. Ibrahim received the B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees in electrical engineering from Ain Shams University, Cairo, Egypt. He is currently an Associate Professor with the Department of Electric Power and Machines, Ain Shams University. He has supervised ten Ph.D. and M.Sc. thesis in the field of electrical power systems. He has taught tens of undergraduate and graduate courses in this field. He has authored and co-authored more than 50 articles. His research interests include distributed generation, control engineering, renewable energy, and power system optimization problems, and power system protection. He can be contacted at email: amr_rizk@eng.asu.edu.eg.
